(1) The quoted sentence exists verbatim at paper/sections/03_filtering.tex:55-59 and pairs the wrong objects: K^{(k)} (q×s mode-conditional gain, eq:gain_k l.238-240) and S^{(k)} (= Σ_{YY,n+1}(k), a mixture over predecessor j, eq:S_k l.235-237) cannot "match" Γ_{jk}/μ_{jk}(·) of Prop 1 (paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex l.109-117), which are pair-indexed kernel statistics — type mismatch both ways, and even the charitable reading S^{(k)}=Γ_{jk} is numerically false (j-mixture with spread-of-means ≠ pair value). The objects that do match Prop 1 are the step-II pair statistics ŷ^{(j,k)}, S^{(j,k)}. (2) I independently re-derived the closed form: under AB, X_{n+1}=Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}Y_{n+1}+(b_{X,k}−Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}b_{Y,k})+W' with Cov(W',V)=0, giving K^{(k)}=Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1} and P^{(k)}=Σ_{U,k}−Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}Δ_k^T at every n (not just stationarity); running scripts/verify_19_20_equivalence.py reproduces agreement at ~1e-16 (max ‖M_a−M_b‖=6.8e-16, ‖Γ_a−Γ_b‖=9.2e-16). Grep confirms ΔΣ_V^{-1} appears in the paper only as the AB parametrisation of (A,B) (04_constraint.tex l.71-73, appendix C l.67), never as the filter gain. (3) The intro (01_introduction.tex l.69-72) does promise "an explicit characterisation of when the two modes agree"; grep shows no such characterisation anywhere — Remark 2 gives only qualitative hedging. (4) "The general (time-varying) mode remains more reliable" (l.62-63) is inverted given the separately confirmed eq:S_jk error, and is further undermined by the paper's own stationary-marginals Remark (02_model_h5.tex rem:marginal_form), under which the h5-exact closed form is exact at all n. No defensible editorial reading exists: the remark states a precise mathematical equality between named symbols that is false under any convention in the paper, and the missing characterisation is a claimed contribution (contribution 1) absent from the body.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep medium. The error sits in a remark rather than a theorem, but it is a false mathematical assertion, the inverted reliability claim misguides practitioners between the two implemented modes, and the unfulfilled "explicit characterisation" promise is a referee-visible gap in a stated contribution. One caveat for aggregation: the reliability-hierarchy sub-claim partially overlaps the already-confirmed eq:S_jk finding and should not be double-counted; the independently novel core (wrong objects + missing closed-form lemma K^{(k)}=Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}, P^{(k)}=Σ_{U,k}−Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}Δ_k^T) justifies medium on its own, especially since the proposed three-line lemma would simultaneously fix the remark and deliver the promised characterisation.

verdicts.title: L'Algorithme 2 idéalise l'implémentation : initialiseur différent d'Alg. 1, alignement k-means non spécifié, l^{(0)} indéfini, l rapportée décalée d'un M-step à I_max

verdict.reasoning: All five sub-claims verified line-by-line against paper/sections/05_estimation.tex and prg/learning/{semi_supervised,supervised}.py. (a) Tex l.167 says "Run Algorithm 1 on (R^(0),X,Y)" but the code calls _initialize_params_from_R (semi_supervised.py l.384-463), a robustified initializer (uniform fallback for empty P rows l.416-419, Laplace-smoothed pi0 l.422-423, F=I / Sigma_W=global cov+0.1I for clusters < dim_z+1 points l.443-447), whereas fit_supervised raises ValueError on empty source/destination regimes (supervised.py l.331-337, l.356-360). (b) Tex l.166 produces N-1 k-means labels while Algorithm 1 (l.37) requires N labels; the code's alignment convention (label of Δz_n → time n+1, R_init[0]=labels[0], semi_supervised.py l.371-373) is absent from the paper. (c) Alg. 2 loop starts at i=1 and l.178 tests |ℓ^(i)−ℓ^(i−1)|<ε, so the first test reads ℓ^(0) which is never defined; the code skips the test at it=0 (l.533). (d) The code evaluates log_lik in the E-step on pre-M-step parameters (l.512-537), so when max_iter is hit without convergence, the returned (theta, log_lik) pair is mismatched by one M-step (params post-last-M-step, ℓ pre-last-M-step, info dict l.642-648), deviating from Alg. 2 l.177/181 which pairs θ^(i) with ℓ^(i)=log p(z|θ^(i)). (e) Tex l.129 says "highest converged log-likelihood" but selection at l.775-781 has no convergence filter — non-converged runs compete. Every cited line number is accurate. These are real paper/code presentation discrepancies; none affect the mathematical validity of the estimator, so the finding is genuine but minor.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep at low — correctly calibrated. The discrepancies are documentation/idealization issues in Algorithm 2's presentation, not mathematical errors; sub-points (d) and (e) could mildly affect reproducibility and restart selection but not the method's correctness.

verdicts.title: Conditionnements différents : la preuve donne p(x_{n+1}|r_{1:n+1}, y_{1:n+1}), les équations cibles conditionnent sur r_{n+1} seul

verdict.reasoning: All factual claims check out against the real .tex. A_time_reversal.tex l.7-11 proves p(x_{n+1}|w_{1:n+1}) = p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}) with w_m=(r_m,y_m), i.e. conditioning on the full regime path r_{1:n+1}; 03_filtering.tex l.209-216 (eq:H5_collapse_mean/var, with \ynp = y_{1:n+1} per macros.tex l.65) conditions on r_{n+1} alone. Taking conditional expectations of the proven identity directly yields E[X_{n+1}|r_{1:n+1}, y_{1:n+1}] = E[X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}, y_{n+1}], whose LHS differs from eq:H5_collapse_mean. Bridging requires marginalising over r_{1:n} given (r_{n+1}, y_{1:n+1}) (or equivalently a tower-property step), which collapses because the inner conditional law is constant in r_{1:n} — valid but nowhere stated; "follow immediately by taking conditional expectations" (l.10-11) does not specify the conditioning and skips this step. The result itself is correct, so this is a rigor/presentation gap, not a mathematical error. The proposed one-sentence fix is correct. Same argument covers the variance (the \Vark macro is in fact a conditional second moment E[XX^T|·], but equality of conditional laws gives equality of all conditional moments).
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep at low. The conclusion of the paper is mathematically correct; only a one-line marginalisation/tower-property justification is missing in the appendix. Fix is a single added sentence.

verdicts.title: Les moments initiaux (mu_z0,k, Sigma_z0,k) sont présentés sous l'étiquette MLE alors qu'ils sont un moment-matching hors vraisemblance

verdict.reasoning: Toutes les citations sont exactes et la dérivation tient. 05_estimation.tex l.17-25 ouvre bien par « Maximum-likelihood estimation factorises across regimes » puis « Sample moments (mu_z0,k, Sigma_z0,k) provide the initial distributions » ; Alg. 1 (fichier l.62-63) prend moyenne/covariance de {z_n : r_n = k} sur toute la trajectoire ; supervised.py l.394-401 fait pareil (np.cov utilise même le diviseur N-1, pas le diviseur MLE). Mathématiquement : theta liste explicitement (mu_z0,k, Sigma_z0,k) comme paramètres (l.9-10), la vraisemblance complète ne contient qu'un facteur p(z_1|r_1) les impliquant, donc leur MLE mono-trajectoire est dégénéré (mu=z_1, Sigma->0) pour k=r_1 et non identifié pour k≠r_1. Les moments empiriques trajectoire-entière estiment la loi marginale intra-régime (~stationnaire), qui ne coïncide avec la loi initiale E[Z_1|r_1=k] (03_filtering.tex l.336-337) qu'à stationnarité — divergence que le papier reconnaît implicitement (Remark h5-exact, 03_filtering l.48-63 : les moments « converge to fixed values », donc varient en transitoire). La partie exonérée (OLS = MLE pour F_k, b_k, Sigma_W,k ; diviseur N_k l.236) est aussi vérifiée. CONTREPOINTS qui calibrent à la baisse : (1) le papier ne dit jamais explicitement que ces moments sont le MLE — il écrit honnêtement « sample moments » ; le rattachement au label MLE est par cadrage de paragraphe, pas par affirmation fausse ; (2) le papier adopte lui-même une initialisation stationnaire (pi_0 = distribution stationnaire du noyau, 03_filtering l.334-335 ; « stationary moments of Z », 02_model_h5 l.128), cadre dans lequel le moment-matching est l'estimateur consistant naturel — l'hypothèse de stationnarité que le correctif demande d'expliciter est déjà à moitié explicite ; (3) pi_0 estimé par fréquences lissées (Alg. 1 l.43-44) n'est pas non plus le MLE mono-trajectoire, signe que le paragraphe décrit une recette standard plutôt qu'une revendication MLE composant par composant. Aucune équation fausse, aucun impact sur les résultats du filtre ; correctif = une phrase.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Baisser de medium à low : imprécision d'exposition réelle (heuristique de moment-matching placée sous la bannière MLE sans la distinguer), mais sans affirmation explicitement fausse, sans équation erronée ni conséquence en aval, et cohérente avec le cadre stationnaire que le papier assume déjà ; correction en une phrase.

verdicts.title: Eqs (21)-(23) : conditionnement \yn = y_{1:n} incohérent — Cov(Y_{n+1},Y_n|·,y_{1:n}) ≡ 0 et Σ_{YY,n}(j)^{-1} = 0^{-1} sous la boucle de la Remarque 4

verdict.reasoning: Every factual citation checks out and the math holds under independent re-derivation. (1) macros.tex:64 defines \yn = y_{1:n}; eqs (22)-(23) (03_filtering.tex:170-183, labels eq:S_jk/eq:M_tilde; numbering (21)-(23) verified by counting numbered displays from section 2) condition the cross-covariances on (j,k,y_{1:n}). Conditional on y_{1:n}, Y_n ≡ y_n a.s., so the centred Cov(Y_{n+1},Y_n|j,k,y_{1:n}) = 0, M̃ ≡ 0, the regression in (21) vanishes, and (22) collapses to Σ_{YY,n+1}(k) — j-independent, contradicting the paper's own Remark 1 (l.56-59) that S matches Γ_{jk}. The centred reading of \Covk is the intended one (eq:cov_Z_Z l.103-104 equates it to F_kΣ_n(j), the centred covariance); under the macro's literal non-centred rendering the formulas are invalid anyway, so the conclusion is robust to that nuance. (2) The symbol overload is textually demonstrable: eq:regime_moments (14) defines (μ_n,P_n) as posterior (|y_{1:n}), while l.119-121 calls the step-(I) output P_{n+1}(k)=E[Z Zᵀ|r_{n+1}=k,y_{1:n}] (predicted) under the same symbol; Remark 4 (l.285-302, quotes at l.294 and l.298-301 accurate) closes the loop with posterior values whose centred YY block is exactly zero, making Σ_{YY,n}(j)^{-1} in (23) the inverse of the zero matrix — the equations are indefinite within the paper's own recursion. (3) Code citation accurate: gss_filter.py l.803-812 uses non-degenerate carried moments with a real regression term, and l.873-874 stores propagated (predicted) moments with no Remark-4 overwrite — the coherent y_{1:n−1}-conditioned reading. (4) Hand re-derivation confirms the mean claim: under the predicted reading, M̃ = C_kM_j + D_k with M_j = Σ_{XY,n}(j)Σ_{YY,n}(j)^{-1} (stationary: Δ_jΣ_{V,j}^{-1} via H5), and (21) reproduces eq:mu_jk (10); only the variance (22) remains wrong, consistent with prior numerical confirmation. No refutation found.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep high, with two caveats: (i) the (22)-variance error itself overlaps the already-confirmed wave-1/2 finding — the new content is the y_{1:n} vs y_{1:n−1} conditioning error and the predicted/posterior symbol overload that renders (23) indefinite (0^{-1}) under the paper's own Remark-4 loop; (ii) the defect is paper-only (notation/exposition in the central mode-update equations of a section claiming exactness) — the code implements the coherent reading, so the fix is editorial: state the conditioning y_{1:n−1} explicitly and introduce distinct predicted/posterior symbols (or move to the posterior formulation ŷ = C_k x̂_{n|n}^{(j)} + D_k y_n + b_{Y,k}, S = C_k P_{n|n}^{(j)} C_kᵀ + Σ_V(k)).

verdicts.title: L'équivalence (H5) ⟺ (H5bis) est annoncée comme prouvée dans l'Appendice A, qui ne la prouve pas

verdict.reasoning: All cited facts verified against the .tex sources. 02_model_h5.tex:46-51 claims (H5) ⟺ (H5bis) "after time reversal, see Appendix A". Appendix A (A_time_reversal.tex) proves only the moment collapse p(x_{n+1}|w_{1:n+1}) = p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}); its sole mention of H5bis (line 22, "cf. eq:H5bis") USES the equivalence as an ingredient of the reverse-time factorisation rather than proving it. Grep confirms the equivalence is proved nowhere else: the only other H5bis reference (04_constraint.tex:21) points to Appendix B, which in fact derives the constraint from (H5) directly, never from (H5bis). Attempted refutations fail: the "Appendix A only hosts the time-reversal machinery" reading collapses because the references are circular (Section 2 cites Appendix A for the equivalence; Appendix A cites eq:H5bis from Section 2 to justify its key factor), so the equivalence is load-bearing yet unproven in the paper's central collapse proof. No companion-paper deferral or stated convention covers it. The equivalence itself is true and elementary (symmetry of conditional independence), which the finding correctly acknowledges — the defect is the misleading/dangling cross-reference, not the mathematics.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Lower from medium to low-medium. Purely expository: the claimed equivalence is true and trivially provable in two lines, the proposed fixes are correct, and nothing downstream (results, constraint, code) is affected. The one aggravating factor keeping it above pure-typo level is the circular cross-reference (Sec. 2 ↔ Appendix A) inside the proof chain of the central collapse result, plus the related mislabel in 04_constraint.tex:21 (cites eq:H5bis while Appendix B actually argues from eq:H5), which the fix should also touch.

verdicts.title: μ_jk omet le terme de biais C_k(b_{X,j} − Δ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹b_{Y,j}) : le noyau (R,Y) du papier est faux dès que b_X ≠ ΔΣ_V⁻¹b_Y

verdict.reasoning: Re-derived the (R,Y) kernel by hand under the paper's own AB parametrisation of (H5): X_n = Δ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹Y_n + c_j + ξ_n with c_j = b_{X,j} − Δ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹b_{Y,j} and ξ_n independent of Y_n given r_n=j, valid for n ≥ 2 regardless of initialization (the intercept comes from the one-step dynamics bias, not marginal means). Hence μ_jk(y_n) = b_{Y,k} + (D_k + C_kΔ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹)y_n + C_k c_j; eq:mu_jk (02_model_h5.tex:109-112) omits C_k c_j while the slope and Γ_jk are correct — exactly as the finding claims. Independently confirmed numerically with a standalone numpy Monte-Carlo (own parameters, 800k trajectories, deliberately non-stationary initialization, transition observed at n=6): empirical intercepts match b_{Y,k}+C_k c_j to MC precision and deviate from the paper's b_{Y,k} by up to 0.66; slopes and residual variances match eq:mu_jk's linear part and eq:Gamma_jk. Adversarial checks found no saving convention: no assumption linking b_X and b_Y anywhere; the kernel is stated for raw variables (it includes b_{Y,k}); Prop. 1 is asserted as exact; and the paper actively reinforces the error with Remark rem:bX_invisible (02_model_h5.tex:120-130) and the abstract claim that b_X is 'invisible in the observable pair' (00_abstract.tex:12-16) — both provably false whenever C_k ≠ 0, which is the paper's raison d'être vs. CGOMSM (where C=0 would make the remark true; likely the source of the error). Confirmed the paper is internally inconsistent: the Sec. 3 recursion (eq:y_pred_jk via μ_n(j), 03_filtering.tex:165-177) and the code (prg/filter/gss_filter.py:452-467) both contain the missing term, so rem:h5exact's 'matching ... μ_jk(·) of Proposition 1' (03_filtering.tex:48-64) only holds with the corrected formula. The proposed fix (add C_k c_j, qualify n=1 under stationary init, align abstract/intro/remarks) is correct; rem:bX_invisible additionally needs to be inverted or deleted.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep critical. A headline proposition of the paper is mathematically false as printed, and the false claim is propagated to the abstract, intro contribution 1, Remark rem:bX_invisible, and rem:h5exact — and it hits precisely the bias extension that is this paper's announced contribution over the unbiased companion. Sole mitigating nuance for the report: the Sec. 3 filter recursion and the code are unaffected (they contain the term), so the fix is a one-term correction plus prose alignment, with no impact on algorithms or experimental results.

verdicts.title: La monotonie EM affirmée n'est pas garantie par le M-step réellement spécifié (moments initiaux, clamp SPD, resets)

verdict.reasoning: Verified by re-deriving Q from the paper's own recursions. App D (l.33-47) defines log alpha_1(k) = log pi_0(k) + iota(k) with iota(k) = log N(z_1; mu_z0_k, Sigma_z0_k), so the complete-data log-likelihood contains exactly ONE term in (mu_z0_k, Sigma_z0_k), weighted gamma_1(k). The M-step actually specified (App D eqs mu_z0_weighted/Sigma_z0_weighted, l.94-102) weights ALL z_n by gamma_n(k) — this maximizes sum_n gamma_n(k) log N(z_n; mu, Sigma), which is NOT a term of Q; the true Q-maximizer is degenerate (mu=z_1, Sigma->0, unbounded). Hence the standard EM monotonicity argument does not apply, and a concrete counterexample exists (theta_old with mu_z0=z_1, Sigma_z0=eps*I at an otherwise stationary point: the update strictly decreases every iota(k), hence strictly decreases log p(z_{1:N}), since the initial-emission factor multiplies every path — confirmed in code, semi_supervised.py l.172 and l.516-519). All cited claims check out verbatim: 05_estimation.tex l.133-135 ("monotone non-decreasing"), abstract l.38, intro l.98, conclusion l.25, and 06_experiments l.322 ("strictly monotone, as guaranteed by theory" — note EM theory never guarantees STRICT monotonicity even with an exact M-step, an additional overstatement at that line; fig caption l.335 says "monotone by construction"). The secondary points are also accurate: SPD clamps (App D l.88-89, l.103) and degenerate-regime resets (l.105-112) deviate from the exact M-step, though only in numerical edge cases. The finding's claim that the remaining updates (P, pi_0, Theta_k, Sigma_W_k) are exact maximizers was independently verified (weighted OLS is the GLS maximizer because the common Sigma_W^{-1} factors out of the normal equations; residual covariance with new Theta is the joint maximizer). Code matches the appendix (semi_supervised.py l.575-584). Attempted refutations failed: iota IS in the monitored likelihood; the initial moments ARE presented inside the M-step (05 l.120-121); the smoothed-initial-moment update is exact only when z_1 is hidden, which is not the case here. The finding is real, precisely cited, and the proposed fixes are correct.
verdict.severityAdjustment: High is defensible for a paper-correctness audit since the false guarantee ("as guaranteed by theory", "monotone by construction") is repeated in five places (abstract, intro, 05, 06, conclusion); medium would also be reasonable because the offending term has weight ~1/N in the likelihood, the empirical curves are monotone in practice, the main contribution (exact filter) is unaffected, and the fix is a local wording change or freezing the initial moments.

verdicts.title: Eq. (22) : variance d'innovation de paire S^{(j,k)} fausse — mélange de conditionnements, contredit la Proposition 1

verdict.reasoning: Finding confirmed after adversarial re-derivation. (1) The conditioning mismatch is unambiguous in the text: paper/sections/03_filtering.tex lines 126-140 define the Sigma-blocks of eq. (22) (eq:S_jk, lines 170-174) as blocks of the centred Sigma_{n+1}(k) from eq:var_Z (lines 106-118), which marginalises over r_n=j via eq:reverse_P and includes a spread-of-means term; the subtracted term is pair-conditional (Covk{...}{j,k,y_{1:n}}). Lines 184-187 confirm no other source for these quantities. (2) The valid Schur complement requires the pair-conditional first term [F_k Sigma_n(j) F_k^T + Sigma_W(k)]_YY; the marginal differs whenever C_k != 0 (the paper's central feature, 02_model_h5.tex lines 78-80), since Gamma_jk (lines 113-116) genuinely depends on j. The identity C_k(Sigma_{U,j} - Delta_j Sigma_{V,j}^{-1} Delta_j^T)C_k^T + Sigma_{V,k} = Gamma_jk checks out exactly against eq:Gamma_jk. (3) Internal contradiction: Lambda is defined at line 147 as p(y_{n+1}|y_n,r_n=j,r_{n+1}=k), exactly the Prop. 1 kernel N(mu_jk(y_n), Gamma_jk) (n-invariant by Markovianity of (R,Y), invoked at lines 148-149), yet eqs (20)-(23) give an n-dependent j-mixed object with no derivation; Remark 1 (lines 48-64) even says the stationary S should match Gamma_jk, which (22) cannot. (4) No editorial defense exists: lines 14-15, 43-46 and 217 claim exactness with no approximation caveat anywhere. (5) Code corroborates: prg/filter/gss_filter.py line 809 implements (22) literally and needs _psd_floor, betraying the non-PSD risk; numerical divergence of imm_general under (H5) (max|dpi|~0.25-0.46) was already established. (6) Aggravating: under the paper's own definitions (eq:regime_moments line 34-39 conditions on y_{1:n}; Remark 4 lines 285-302 feeds posterior moments where Y_n=y_n is degenerate), Sigma_{YY,n}(j)=0 and eq:M_tilde (lines 179-183) inverts a zero matrix - the (21)-(23) block is not even evaluable under stated conventions. This is an error of statement, not an assumed approximation.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep critical. The error sits in the mode-probability update of the main recursion, contaminates pi_{n+1} and the subsequent steps (I)/(IV), and directly falsifies the paper's headline exactness claim for the general (time-varying) mode - the mode Remark 1 recommends in transient/non-stationary settings. Sole mitigation: the h5-exact stationary mode bypasses (22) via Gamma_jk and remains correct, so the contribution survives once (22) is fixed (pair-conditional predictive variance, or directly invoking Prop. 1's Gamma_jk under H5).

verdicts.title: Complexité jamais quantifiée : aucun O(K²) dans le papier ; seule affirmation « operations independent of n » (correcte)

verdict.reasoning: Every factual claim verified against the real .tex. (1) Exhaustive grep confirms zero occurrences of "O(K", "complexity", or "quadratic" in paper/sections/, paper/appendix/, paper.tex. (2) paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex l.159-161 contains the paper's only operations statement: "in a number of operations independent of n" — itself correct. (3) The cited exactness/speed claims exist verbatim at the stated lines: 03_filtering.tex l.4 (title "Optimal filtering recursion"), l.13-15 ("this IMM is exact"), l.43-46 ("no Gaussian-collapse approximation is made at any stage"); 08_conclusion.tex l.10 sells "a fast, exact optimal filter". (4) The only per-step quantification anywhere is empirical wall-clock timing (06_experiments.tex l.153, 166-167: 309 vs 425 µs/step), never asymptotic — so the narrow claim "no asymptotic complexity stated" holds. (5) The proposed O(K²s³ + K(q+s)³) per-step figure is sound at leading order: Step II (l.142-200) computes K² pair likelihoods with s×s innovation covariances S^{(j,k)} (eqs. indexed by pair (j,k), mode update sums over j',k'); Steps I/III are K propagations/Kalman updates in dimension q+s. Lower-order terms (O(K²(q+s)²) mixing in Step I) exist but don't change the picture. (6) The cross-reference to the eq:S_jk conditioning inconsistency (l.170-175: marginal Σ_YY,n+1(k) from eq:var_Z combined with pair-conditional cross-covariance) matches the printed text and the previously confirmed critical finding. The finding is real, precisely evidenced, and its low severity is appropriate for a completeness/presentation gap rather than a mathematical error.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep at low. It is an editorial completeness gap (missing asymptotic complexity statement in a paper whose selling point includes speed), not an error. One nuance for the report: the paper does quantify speed empirically (309 µs/step, 06_experiments.tex l.166), so phrase the finding as "no asymptotic/big-O complexity stated" rather than "complexity never quantified". The exactness-contradiction remark belongs to the separate critical finding on eq:S_jk and should not inflate this one's severity.

verdicts.title: Argument d'exactitude de l'étape (III) erroné : la prédictive p(X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n}) N'EST PAS « déjà une seule gaussienne »

verdict.reasoning: Verified verbatim against paper/sections/03_filtering.tex:217-228 and paper/macros.tex: \yn = y_{1:n} and line 224 uses bare y_{n+1} (not \ynp = y_{1:n+1}, which IS used correctly at lines 210-216, ruling out a notational convention). The claim that the predictive mixture p(X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n}) "is already a single Gaussian, namely p(X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1})" is mathematically false on both counts in the finding: (i) conditioning sets differ — the RHS is a function of y_{n+1}, absent from the LHS's conditioning; (ii) decomposing the predictive as the y_{n+1}-mixture of p(x_{n+1}|k,y_{n+1}) weighted by p(y_{n+1}|k,y_{1:n}) (itself a j-mixture with distinct means via mu_n(j), eqs (13)-(15)) shows it is a genuine Gaussian mixture under H5. Likewise the joint p(Z_{n+1}|k,y_{1:n}) at lines 226-228 is a mixture (component means F_k mu_n(j)+b_k differ in j), so calling the update "a Gaussian conditioning" of it is wrong as stated — moment-based linear conditioning of a non-Gaussian joint is not generically exact. I re-derived the finding's repair argument and it is correct: Appendix A's strong collapse p(x_{n+1}|w_{1:n+1})=p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}) gives every component the same conditional N(A_k+M_k y, G_k); then Sigma_XY = M_k Sigma_YY and Sigma_XX = G_k + M_k Sigma_YY M_k^T (total variance), so (27)-(31) exactly recover the true posterior. Grep over sections/ and appendix/ confirms this bridging argument appears nowhere in the paper. Not a defensible editorial shorthand: it is the bolded "key feature that makes the IMM exact" sentence, the paper's only main-text justification of step (III) exactness, and Appendix A proves only the posterior collapse (eqs 25-26), not anything about the predictive. Mitigating: equations (27)-(31), the algorithm, and the code are correct, and exactness is independently established by Appendix A plus the cited companion paper (the section presents itself as a re-expression, lines 19-22).
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep high (or at minimum medium-high). It is a false statement inside the bolded central exactness argument of the paper — a reviewer would flag it immediately and it is the only main-text justification of step (III). However, it does not invalidate any equation, result, or code: the recursion is exact and independently grounded in Appendix A and the companion paper. The fix is the two-line proof proposed in the finding (per-component shared conditional + law of total variance), which I verified is correct.

verdicts.title: Sélection multi-restart sur la vraisemblance PRÉ-projection : le papier décrit fidèlement le code mais n'avertit jamais que l_hat ne correspond pas au modèle retourné

verdict.reasoning: Every factual claim verified against the sources. 05_estimation.tex Alg. 2 l.183 selects θ̂ = argmax ℓ over restarts, then l.184-186 overwrites A_k, B_k via the AB projection; l.129 retains the restart with the 'highest converged log-likelihood' — a pre-projection quantity. Neither §5 nor Appendix D states that log p(z|θ̂_projected) differs from the reported ℓ̂, nor that the restart ranking could change post-projection. Code matches: semi_supervised.py records info['log_lik'] (l.643) from the last EM iterate BEFORE the post-hoc projection (l.589-616), selects on it (l.779), and writes 'log L=best_log_lik' into the saved projected model's docstring (l.952-955). The refutation attempt fails because of an aggravating instance the finding did not cite: figures/generated/tab_enso_em.tex reports train log L̂ = −437.4 for the V1 post-hoc-AB row — the pre-projection value attributed in a results table to the projected model, whose likelihood provably differs (V1 and V0 differ on test NLL, +0.132 vs +0.177, so they are different models). So this is not merely a missing caveat; a published number is attributed to the wrong model. Defensibility check: the procedure itself (project-the-MLE, select on unconstrained ℓ) is standard, fully disclosed in Alg. 2 and §5.2.4, and 07_real_data.tex l.154-157 partially explains why V0/V1 share the training value, which mitigates intent but does not fix the table semantics. The ranking-inversion concern (point ii) is real in principle but empirically moot in the paper's own experiments (tab_em_restarts: 100% basin agreement with one restart on M1).
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep medium, but at the lower edge. Point (ii) (restart re-ranking) should be presented as a theoretical caveat only — the paper's own restart study shows it cannot have affected the reported results. The strongest concrete element is not the missing warning in §5.2.4 (the timing is openly disclosed there and in Alg. 2) but the misattributed 'train log L̂' for V1 in tab_enso_em (§7.3) and the code-generated docstring; the recommended fix (recompute log p(z|θ̂_projected) with one forward pass and report it, plus one caveat sentence in §5.2.4) is correct and cheap.

verdicts.title: (H3), 2e partie : conditionnement sur (x_n, y_n) manquant — telle qu'écrite, l'hypothèse contredit le modèle qu'elle définit

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the actual .tex and code. (1) Lines 23-24 of paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex literally state the marginal version p(x_{n+1},y_{n+1}|r_n,r_{n+1}) = p(x_{n+1},y_{n+1}|r_{n+1}); \pk in macros.tex (line 38) is a plain conditional density, no implicit z_n conditioning. (2) The derivation gap is real: under H1+H2 the pair-conditional transition has matrices F(j,k), b(j,k), Q(j,k); collapsing to the single-index form of eq:dynamics (lines 58-62) is equivalent to the CONDITIONAL statement p(z_{n+1}|z_n,r_n,r_{n+1}) = p(z_{n+1}|z_n,r_{n+1}); the marginal statement does not imply it (explicit counterexample: j-dependent F(j,k) with b(j,k), Q(j,k) chosen to match marginals). (3) The contradiction is real: given eq:dynamics and H3's first equation (which yields p(z_n|r_n,r_{n+1}) = p(z_n|r_n)), the written marginal H3 forces F_k(mu_Z(j)-mu_Z(j')) = 0 and F_k(Sigma_Z(j)-Sigma_Z(j'))F_k^T = 0 for all j,j', i.e. identical per-regime moments for invertible F_k — degenerate. (4) The paper contradicts its own written H3 downstream: Prop. 1 (eq:mu_jk, eq:Gamma_jk, lines 109-117) makes the law of y_{n+1} given (j,k) j-dependent, and Appendix B line 70 explicitly defines Sigma_{YY,n+1}(k,j) = Var(Y_{n+1}|r_n=j,r_{n+1}=k) with a j index — a quantity the written H3 would make j-free. (5) The code confirms: prg/filter/gss_filter.py _precompute_stationary (lines 330-350) computes mu(k) = F_k * sum_j p_rev[j,k] mu(j) + b_k (mixture over predecessor regimes, needed precisely because E[Z_{n+1}|j,k] depends on j), and line 458 computes mu_Y_jk = F_k[Y-rows] @ mu_z[j] + b_Y — the exact j-dependent quantity the written H3 forbids; per-regime moments are distinct. The proposed fix (condition both sides on x_n, y_n) is the standard CGPMSM-type assumption and is what all downstream derivations implicitly use. All line/equation citations in the finding check out.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep high. It is a definitional error in the foundational assumption set: as written, (H1)-(H3) cannot deliver eq:dynamics, and (H3) literally contradicts the paper's own Prop. 1, Appendix B, and the implemented model. Mitigating nuance for the report: the error is confined to the STATEMENT of (H3) — all downstream derivations implicitly use the correct conditional version, so no result is invalidated and the fix is a one-line edit (add conditioning on x_n, y_n to both sides).

verdicts.title: Équivalence (H5) ⟺ (H5bis) attribuée à un « time reversal » et à l'App. A : c'est la simple symétrie de l'indépendance conditionnelle

verdict.reasoning: All factual claims check out against the real .tex. (1) 02_model_h5.tex l.46-47 does say the H5/H5bis equivalence holds "(after time reversal, see Appendix A)". (2) Rederivation confirms (H5) [p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1},w_n)=p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1})] and (H5bis) [p(w_n|w_{n+1},x_{n+1})=p(w_n|w_{n+1})] are the same conditional independence X_{n+1} ⫫ W_n | W_{n+1}; the equivalence is one line of Bayes (positive Gaussian densities under H2), needing no time-reversal machinery. (3) Appendix A (A_time_reversal.tex l.4, 7-9) proves the different, stronger collapse p(x_{n+1}|w_{1:n+1})=p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}), and at l.21-22 it ASSUMES the H5/H5bis identification ("is the time-reversed counterpart of (H5) (cf. eq:H5bis)") rather than proving it — so the section-02 pointer is circular as well as misplaced. Adversarial checks: the claimed equivalence is mathematically true (so no result is wrong, only the justification/cross-reference), and grep confirms no other place in the paper proves it (04_constraint.tex l.21 even cites H5bis where App. B l.8 starts from H5, showing the paper treats them as interchangeable). Severity [low] is well calibrated: a misleading attribution/cross-reference, trivially fixable as the finding proposes.
verdict.severityAdjustment: none — [low] is correct (presentation/cross-reference defect; the underlying mathematical claim is true)

verdicts.title: Macros \Covk/\Vark définies comme moments NON centrés mais utilisées comme covariances centrées — eq. (17) littéralement fausse sous la définition de la macro

verdict.reasoning: Every factual element of the finding checks out against the real sources. (1) paper/macros.tex:22-26 defines \Covk{A}{B}{c} = E[A Bᵀ|c] and \Vark{A}{c} = E[A Aᵀ|c] — non-centred second moments — while the comments call them covariances; no \renewcommand overrides them anywhere. (2) Equation numbering verified: section 02 has 13 numbered equations, so eq:cov_Z_Z (03_filtering.tex:103-105) is indeed eq. (17), eq:mix_var is (13), eq:noise_cov is (8), eq:var_Z is (18). (3) I redid the derivation for (17): the literal LHS E[Z_{n+1}Z_nᵀ|j,k,y_{1:n}] = F_k P_n(j) + b_k μ_n(j)ᵀ (using H3 and zero-mean W), whereas the printed RHS F_k(P_n(j) − μ_n(j)μ_n(j)ᵀ) is the centred covariance; the gap (F_k μ_n(j)+b_k)μ_n(j)ᵀ is non-zero in general and the paper insists b_r ≠ 0 is first-class (02_model_h5.tex:80-82). So (17) as printed is false. Aggravating detail: lines 119-121 say (18) "follows from (16)–(17)", yet (18) is correct under the NON-centred reading (verified term by term) while (17) needs the centred one — adjacent equations use opposite conventions. (4) P_{n|n}^{(k)} := \Vark{X_n}{r_n=k,y_{1:n}} (l.31-32) is then computed as the centred Σ_XX − K S Kᵀ (l.247-250, Σ-blocks declared centred at l.137-140) and combined with a spread-of-means term — inconsistent with its printed non-centred definition. (5) Eq. (13) (02_model_h5.tex:152-157): under the non-centred reading the spread term must be absent (E[XXᵀ|y] = Σπ E[XXᵀ|r,y]); the printed equation is only true for centred Var. (6) l.337 manual centring \Vark{Z_1 − μ_{z0}(k)}{r_1=k} confirmed verbatim, and l.363 adds μμᵀ back — proof the macro is used non-centred elsewhere, so the rendered E[··ᵀ|·] notation is genuinely ambiguous for the reader (eq. (14) uses it in the true non-centred sense). The defense "it's only macro internals, intent is clear" fails: the PDF prints E[·] notation used in both senses within the same section. The inconsistency also propagates to eq:S_jk/eq:M_tilde (l.170-183) and appendices B and E. The proposed fix (centred Cov/Var macros + a distinct second-moment macro for (14)/(18), drop the manual centring at l.337) is correct. Severity: medium is well calibrated — several printed equations are literally false as displayed, which a referee would flag, but the defect is notational/presentational: the intended math is right under the centred reading and the algorithm and code (prg/filter/gss_filter.py) are unaffected.
verdict.severityAdjustment: keep medium — pervasive notation bug making printed equations (esp. eq. 17) literally false, but no algorithmic or code impact; fix is purely editorial

verdicts.title: Remarque 1 : la factorisation est triviale et la gaussianité de p(x_n, y_n | r_n) est fausse en général

verdict.reasoning: La trouvaille repose sur une lecture incomplète de (H3). Dans 02_model_h5.tex:21-25, (H3) comporte DEUX équations, et la seconde — p(x_{n+1},y_{n+1}|r_n,r_{n+1}) = p(x_{n+1},y_{n+1}|r_{n+1}) — est une condition MARGINALE (sans conditionnement sur z_n), i.e. Z_{n+1} ⊥ R_n | R_{n+1}. Sous les hypothèses telles qu'imprimées, la gaussianité de p(z_n|r_n) se DÉMONTRE par récurrence : (i) cas de base : (H3)-1 + (H1) donnent z_1 ⊥ r_{2:N} | r_1, donc p(z_1|r_1) = p(z_1|r_{1:N}), gaussienne par (H2) ; (ii) hérédité : p(z_{n+1}|r_{1:n+1}) = ∫ p(z_n|r_n) N(z_{n+1}; F_{r_{n+1}}z_n+b, Σ_W) dz_n = p(z_{n+1}|r_n,r_{n+1}) — gaussienne unique — qui égale p(z_{n+1}|r_{n+1}) par (H3)-2. Le « contre-argument structurel » de la trouvaille (mélange p(z_2|r_2) à K composantes distinctes p(z_2|r_1=j,r_2) dépendant de j) VIOLE précisément (H3)-2 : ce n'est pas un contre-exemple dans la classe de modèles du papier. L'affirmation « aucune des hypothèses (H1)–(H3), (H5) ne le réduit à une gaussienne » est donc mathématiquement fausse pour le (H3) imprimé. L'appendice B (B_h5_derivation.tex:14-15 : « By (H2)–(H3), the conditional law p(z_n,z_{n+1}|r_n=j,r_{n+1}=k) is Gaussian ») confirme que le papier lui-même lit (H3) au niveau marginal — lecture standard dans la littérature CGOMSM/CGPMSM stationnaire citée. Restes valides mais mineurs : la clause de factorisation seule est en effet la règle de chaînage (trivial), et « stationary in the form of its marginals » est informel — quibbles de rédaction, pas une erreur mathématique [high] ; et la Remarque 1 n'est d'ailleurs référencée nulle part ailleurs (non porteuse). NOTE pour l'audit (trouvaille DIFFÉRENTE, non celle soumise) : (H3)-2 imprimé + paramétrage k-seul de eq:dynamics (F_k inversible) force des marginales continues identiques entre régimes, alors que le code (gss_filter.py:329-380) calcule des moments stationnaires dépendant du régime — c'est la formulation de (H3) qui mériterait un examen, pas la Remarque 1 prise sous les hypothèses écrites. La trouvaille soumise, elle, conclut à tort que les hypothèses n'impliquent pas la gaussianité.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Si on tenait à garder quelque chose : sévérité low, recentrée sur la rédaction (clause de factorisation triviale, locution « stationary in the form of its marginals » informelle) — mais le cœur mathématique de la trouvaille (gaussianité « fausse en général » sous (H1)–(H3)+(H5)) est réfuté par la seconde équation de (H3) telle qu'imprimée.

verdicts.title: Cas dégénérés passés sous silence : inversibilité de S^{(k)}, Σ_{YY,0}(k), Σ_{XX,n+1}(k) (Joseph) et Σ_{V,j} jamais hypothéquée

verdict.reasoning: Every factual claim verified against the real .tex and compiled paper.aux. (i) eq (29) (l.238-241) inverts S^(k)=Σ_YY,n+1(k) with no invertibility hypothesis; (ii) eqs (35)-(37) (l.345-360) invert Σ_YY,0(k) (K_1 at l.351); (iii) Remark on Joseph form (l.256-283, eq 32) defines H^(k):=Σ_YX Σ_XX^{-1} at l.273 — I re-derived the algebra: (32) does reduce to the short form (31) for K=Σ_XY S^{-1}, but requires Σ_XX invertible, and R^(k) is the Schur complement (PSD only if the joint is PSD); Appendix E (l.33) repeats the definition with no condition; (iv) Σ_{V,j}^{-1} appears in eqs (10)-(11) of 02_model_h5.tex (l.111-115) with no hypothesis, while §4 (04_constraint.tex l.39-40, 58-59) and App. C (l.25-26, 73) do state ≻0 but never link back to §3; (v) l.334-335 says "the stationary distribution", presupposing uniqueness; (vi) the paper is silent on the denominator of (24) while the code (prg/filter/gss_filter.py) guards via logsumexp (l.570, 640, 733), _safe_solve, _psd_floor, allow_singular — all confirmed present. Grep confirms no positive-definiteness/invertibility assumption anywhere in §2-§3. Nothing in (H1)-(H3),(H5) precludes singular Σ_{V,k} or Σ_XX, so the gap is genuine, not implied. Two minor errors in the finding that do not invalidate it: the Joseph remark is compiled as Remark 5, not "Remark 3" (global remark counter; line range and eq number cited are correct, referent unambiguous); and point (v) overstates the needed condition — on a finite state space existence is automatic and uniqueness needs only irreducibility, aperiodicity being required only for convergence (relevant to Remark 4's stationarity claim, not to π_0 itself).
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep [low] — correctly calibrated. Real but standard exposition gap (implicit regularity assumptions common in applied filtering papers), fixable with one sentence at the head of §3; slightly aggravated by the asymmetry that §4/App. C do state ≻0 hypotheses while §3 and Prop. 1 never do, and by the code needing explicit guards (_safe_solve, _psd_floor, allow_singular) the paper never mentions.

verdicts.title: Prop. 1 : homogénéité non justifiée (loi initiale jamais spécifiée) et appui circulaire sur la « noise law » de l'App. B

verdict.reasoning:
All factual claims check out against the real .tex and code; I also re-derived the math and it supports the finding.

(a) Homogeneity/initialisation gap — CONFIRMED. The kernel p(r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1} | r_n=j, y_n) = P_{j,k} ∫ N(y_{n+1}; C_k x_n + D_k y_n + b_{Y,k}, Σ_{V,k}) p(x_n | r_n=j, y_n) dx_n, so eq:mu_jk/eq:Gamma_jk (02_model_h5.tex l. 109-117) hold iff p(x_n | r_n=j, y_n) = N(Δ_jΣ_{V,j}^{-1}y_n, Σ_{U,j} − Δ_jΣ_{V,j}^{-1}Δ_j^T), i.e. the CENTERED noise-law conditional, at every n including n=1. I verified the finding's non-trivial sub-claim: under AB (A_k=Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}C_k, B_k=Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}D_k, eq:AB in 04_constraint.tex l. 69-76), X_{n+1} = Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}Y_{n+1} + c_k + ξ_{n+1} with c_k = b_{X,k} − Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}b_{Y,k} and ξ_{n+1} ⊥ (V_{n+1}, Z_n, past), Var(ξ)=Σ_{U,k} − Δ_kΣ_{V,k}^{-1}Δ_k^T, so p(x_m|r_m,y_m) has the noise-law form (with intercept c_j) for all m ≥ 2 regardless of initialisation — but the n=1→2 transition does require p(x_1|r_1,y_1) to already have that form. Section 2 indeed never defines the transition matrix (P_{j,k} first appears undefined at l. 105; defined only in 03_filtering.tex l. 156) nor any initial law; grep confirms no p(r_1)/p(z_1|r_1) in 02_model_h5.tex. Sec. 3.5 (03_filtering.tex l. 330-366) takes π_0 = stationary distribution but leaves μ_{z0}(k), Σ_{z0}(k) arbitrary ("the model provides"), so the assumption needed for Prop. 1's homogeneity from n=1 is stated nowhere. The code confirms the implicit assumption: gss_filter.py l. 532-539 comment block says the h5_exact init "uses the *stationary* prior ... ensures the filter starts in steady state", exactly as the finding reports. Prop. 1's proof is deferred to the companion paper (l. 96-98), which mitigates but does not remove the in-paper gap.

(b) Noise-law justification — CONFIRMED. App. B l. 23-26 says verbatim that "The Markovianity argument of Appendix A ... shows that all quantities relevant to (H5) may be evaluated with the noise law, i.e. with Z_n | r_n = j having mean zero and covariance P(j)"; App. A (l. 7-33) proves only the collapse p(x_{n+1}|w_{1:n+1}) = p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}) and says nothing about marginal moments, so the citation does not support the claim. Section 4 itself (04_constraint.tex l. 51-65) silently equates the noise covariance Σ(r) with "second-order statistics of (X_n, Y_n) | r_n = r", confirming the unstated marginal-equals-noise-law assumption. The intercept slip is real: under the centered noise law with bias, E[Y_n]=0, E[Y_{n+1}]=b_{Y,k}, E[X_{n+1}]=b_{X,k}, so the intercept in eq:mean_X_linear (App. B l. 51-54) is b_{X,k} − β₂ b_{Y,k}, not b_{X,k}; as the finding correctly notes this does not affect β₁=0 or the final remark. And the centered ("mean zero") evaluation is exactly what produces the missing C_k c_j term in eq:mu_jk in the biased case (the separate critical finding), which my AB derivation independently reproduces.

Nothing in the finding is overstated: it correctly attributes the non-trivial n ≥ 2 robustness to AB, correctly limits the App-B intercept issue to "no consequence for β₁=0", and the proposed fixes are apt. Severity medium is well calibrated: it is a rigor/justification gap (Prop. 1 as stated is not self-contained and is false at n=1 for non-stationary initialisation; the substantive biased-μ_jk error is tracked by the separate critical finding).
verdict.severityAdjustment: keep medium

verdicts.title: Objectif de filtrage défini avec y_{1:N} (lissage) au lieu de y_{1:n} (filtrage)

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly in the sources. paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex:144 defines, under the subsection "The filtering problem", \hat X_n := \Ek{X_n}{\yN}, and paper/macros.tex:64-66 confirms \yN renders as y_{1:N} (smoother) while \yn renders y_{1:n} (filter). Adversarial checks all fail to rescue the text: (a) \yN appears exactly once in the whole paper (this line), so it is not an announced convention; (b) the same sentence continues with eq:mix_mean/eq:mix_var (lines 148-158) conditioning on \ynp = y_{1:n+1}, and line 159 states the filter propagates p(r_{n+1}|y_{1:n+1}) — the shown decomposition computes E[X_{n+1}|y_{1:n+1}], which equals E[X_n|y_{1:N}] only at n=N; (c) section 03_filtering.tex (e.g. line 31) defines all targets with \yn/\ynp; (d) decisively, 08_conclusion.tex:34-35 defers "exact smoothing" to future work, so the paper cannot intend a smoothing objective. This is a genuine \yN-for-\yn typo, and the proposed one-token fix is correct.
verdict.severityAdjustment: medium → low-medium. Real and worth fixing — it misstates the paper's central objective in a paper whose contribution hinges on the filter/smoother distinction (smoothing is explicitly future work). But it is a single-token typo, used nowhere else, non-propagating into any derivation, and the intended meaning is unambiguous from the equations three lines below. "low" would also be defensible; "medium" is the ceiling.

verdicts.title: Distribution stationnaire NON requise (correct) ; (H2)-(H3) invoqués mais inutilisés ; wording « collapses into the constant 1 » imprécis

verdict.reasoning: Re-derived the appendix A proof independently and verified every factual claim against /Users/MacBook_Derrode/Documents/ProjetsRecherche/Markov/FofGss/exactIMM/paper/appendix/A_time_reversal.tex. (1) Stationarity claim: correct — no occurrence of 'stationary' in the file; the proof uses exact backward conditional densities p(w_m|w_{m+1}), p(x_m|w_m,w_{m+1},x_{m+1}) which are valid even though the time-reversed chain is inhomogeneous; no stationary law or homogeneous reversed kernel is needed or claimed. (2) Hypothesis usage: correct — the factorization (l.15-20) needs only (H1) (backward Markov property of the triplet) plus (H5bis) applied at each step; the division step (l.31-32) does NOT secretly require Prop. prop:markov (H2-H3): the denominator factorization of p(w_{1:n+1}) follows by integrating the derived multline over x_{n+1}. (H2)/(H3) appear only in the statement at l.7, never in a proof step; H5⟺H5bis is bare conditional-independence symmetry. Only caveat: (H2) implicitly underwrites existence of densities/second moments, so 'unused' is a hair strong — but the finding itself already proposes keeping (H1)-(H3) as harmless, so this does not change the verdict. (3) Wording nit: correct — the second bracket (l.18-19) includes p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}), which survives marginalization over x_1..x_n; the bracket collapses to p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1}), not 'the constant 1' (l.23-25), while the displayed multline (l.26-30) is mathematically correct. (4) The corollary eq:H5_collapse_mean/var (03_filtering.tex l.209-216) does follow immediately by tower property. All cited line numbers check out.
verdict.severityAdjustment: None — [info] is the right level. This is a positive verification (no stationarity needed, proof correct) plus two cosmetic nits (surplus hypotheses in the statement, one imprecise sentence whose displayed equation is nonetheless correct). The proposed optional fixes are accurate; if anything, prefer the second fix ('collapses the second bracket into p(x_{n+1}|w_{n+1})') over changing l.7, since (H2) implicitly guarantees the densities/second moments used by the notation.

verdicts.title: Énoncé faux : « for any matrix K » avec membre de gauche Var[X_{n+1} | r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1}]

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed against the actual source. paper/appendix/E_joseph.tex lines 53-55 state the Joseph identity holds "for any matrix K (not only the optimal Kalman gain)", and eq:joseph_proof (lines 56-61) has LHS \Vark{X_{n+1}}{r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1}}, which per macros.tex:25-26 and uniform usage in the paper (e.g. 03_filtering.tex:32) is the conditional posterior covariance Var[X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1}] — a fixed quantity independent of K. Independent algebraic re-derivation: with H=Σ_YX Σ_XX^{-1}, the RHS expands to Σ_XX − KΣ_YX − Σ_XY Kᵀ + KSKᵀ = Var(X|Y) + (K−K*)S(K−K*)ᵀ with K*=Σ_XY S^{-1}; since S≻0, equality with the LHS holds iff K=K*. Independent numerical test (fresh code, q=3, s=2, SPD joint covariance) reproduces the finding: optimal K gives agreement to 8.9e-16 with both the short form and Var(X|Y); arbitrary K gives discrepancy ≈9.59 with Var(X|Y) but agrees to 3.6e-15 with the error covariance of the K-gain affine estimator. The correct "for any K" statement requires the LHS to be E[(X−x̂)(X−x̂)ᵀ|·] with x̂=μ_X+K(y−μ_Y), exactly as the finding says, matching the textbook (Anderson-Moore) formulation. Impact is correctly characterized as contained: main-text eq:joseph (03_filtering.tex:261-267) uses K defined as the optimal gain (eq:gain_k) and is correct; the equivalence subsection E_joseph.tex:64-75 is correct (re-verified); the code uses only the optimal gain. The proposed fixes are sound.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Lower from high to medium. The error is mathematically real and sits in a displayed equation of a paper whose selling point is exactness, so a referee could flag it — but it is confined to a parenthetical claim plus a mislabeled LHS in an appendix remark, the equation is true at the gain actually used everywhere (paper and code), nothing propagates, and the fix is a one-line rewording.

verdicts.title: Formulations à polir : « each pair as a single observation » (paires chevauchantes) et « converged blocks » dans le cas supervisé one-shot

verdict.reasoning: Both sub-claims verified against the real .tex and code. (1) paper/sections/05_estimation.tex l.75-79 does say "Treating each pair (Z_n, Z_{n+1}) as a single observation conditioned on R_{n+1}"; this is genuinely imprecise prose: overlapping pairs sharing Z_n cannot be conditionally independent HMM observations (a joint pair-emission p(z_n,z_{n+1}|r_{n+1}) would double-count each z_n). The structure actually used — and stated in the very next formula — is the autoregressive-HMM factorization p(z_1|r_1)·prod p(z_{n+1}|z_n,r_{n+1}), which is what justifies forward-backward. I re-derived eq:forward/eq:backward (l.84-93), App D recursions (l.37-47), posteriors (l.52-58), the identity sum_k xi_n(j,k)=gamma_n(j) (l.59-60, holds for n<N), and eq:log_mvn (l.19-24): all correct and matching _forward/_backward/_compute_xi/_log_mvn_batch in prg/learning/semi_supervised.py l.111-212, confirming the finding's claim that only the wording (not the math) is at fault. (2) l.26-28 does say "to the converged (C_k, D_k, Delta_k, Sigma_V,k) blocks" in the supervised subsection, where estimation is one-shot closed-form OLS (Algorithm 1) with no iteration — "converged" is a leftover from the EM vocabulary (used correctly at l.129/l.133). The clamp-SPD-then-AB order of Alg 1 l.56-59 matches prg/learning/supervised.py l.253-261. Proposed fixes are accurate and minimal. Severity [info] is correctly calibrated: pure wording polish, zero mathematical or implementation impact.
verdict.severityAdjustment: None — [info] is the right level; this is prose polish with no effect on correctness of derivations or code.

verdicts.title: Objectif de filtrage défini avec y_{1:N} (lissage) au lieu de y_{1:n} (filtrage)

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly in the sources. macros.tex l. 17 (\Ek{A}{c} → E[A|c]) and l. 64-66 (\yn → y_{1:n}, \ynp → y_{1:n+1}, \yN → y_{1:N}) match the finding exactly. 02_model_h5.tex l. 140-144: the subsection titled "The filtering problem" defines \hat X_n := \Ek{X_n}{\yN} = E[X_n | y_{1:N}], which for n < N is the fixed-interval smoother, not the filter. The immediately following decomposition eq:mix_mean/eq:mix_var (l. 148-158) and the closing text (l. 159-160) condition on \ynp = y_{1:n+1} and describe propagating filtered quantities, so the stated recursion cannot compute the stated objective. Attempted refutations failed: no smoothing content exists in sections 02/03 (grep "smooth" empty); Section 3 conditions exclusively on \yn/\ynp; \yN occurs exactly once in sections 02-03, at the flagged line, so it is an isolated typo rather than a convention. The proposed fix (\Ek{X_n}{\yn}) is correct.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Lower from medium to low. \hat X_n is never referenced again anywhere in sections/ or appendix/, and every downstream derivation correctly conditions on y_{1:n+1}, so the typo has no mathematical consequence — it is a one-macro fix. Still worth correcting because it misdefines the paper's central objective in the problem-statement subsection.

verdicts.title: La monotonie EM affirmée n'est pas garantie par le M-step réellement spécifié (moments initiaux, clamp SPD, resets)

verdict.reasoning: Verified line by line. (1) The likelihood the paper claims is monotone genuinely depends on the initial moments: App D l.35-38 defines iota(k)=log N(z_1; mu_z0,k, Sigma_z0,k) inside alpha_1, and the code's monitored log_lik includes log_init (semi_supervised.py l.517-523, l.172). (2) The M-step specified in App D l.94-102 (gamma_n(k)-weighted moments over ALL n; code l.575-584) does not maximize the actual Q-term, which is gamma_1(k) log N(z_1; mu', Sigma') — a single factor. It maximizes the Q of a different model (i.i.d. emissions), so the EM ascent inequality does not cover the joint update; moreover the true Q-term has sup=+infinity (mu'=z_1, Sigma'->0), so an exact M-step does not even exist, meaning the claim cannot be repaired without freezing these parameters or conditioning on z_1. (3) The monotonicity claim is unqualified and repeated 6 times (05 l.133-135, abstract l.38, intro l.98, 06 l.322 'strictly monotone, as guaranteed by theory', fig caption l.335 'monotone by construction', conclusion l.25); no caveat exists anywhere about the initial-moment heuristic, SPD clamps (App D l.88-89, 103) or degenerate-regime resets (l.105-112). (4) The finding's concessions check out: the P, pi_0, Theta_k, Sigma_W,k updates are exact coordinate maximizers. Adversarial readings fail: 06 l.322 elevates the statement to a theorem, so it is not a defensible informal contrast with GEM. The finding is real and not a misreading. Severity is overstated, however: the deviation is confined to parameters contributing O(1) to an O(N) log-likelihood, all other updates are exact ascent steps, any tiny decrease would merely trigger the |delta|<tol stop, the reported empirical monotonicity is plausibly genuine, and the paper's central exact-filtering results are unaffected. It is a false theoretical guarantee fixable by a one-sentence qualification or by freezing the initial moments — a presentation-level correctness issue, not a result-invalidating one.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Lower from high to medium: the mathematical gap is real and the 'guaranteed by theory' wording is wrong, but it affects only the O(1) initial-moment terms of the likelihood, has negligible practical effect on the experiments, and is fixed by qualifying the claim (05 l.133-135, 06 l.322, abstract/intro/conclusion) or freezing (mu_z0, Sigma_z0) during EM.

verdicts.title: Argument d'exactitude de l'étape (III) erroné : la prédictive p(X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n}) N'EST PAS « déjà une seule gaussienne »

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the real .tex. (1) Citations check out: the disputed text is at paper/sections/03_filtering.tex:217-228; equation numbering confirmed by counting (sections 00-01 have no numbered equations, section 02 gives (1)-(13)), so the H5 collapse eqs are (25)-(26) and the Kalman update is (27)-(31), with the text between them. Macros confirm \yn=y_{1:n}, \ynp=y_{1:n+1}. (2) The sentence at l.222-224 is mathematically false as written: (H5) (02_model_h5.tex:40-45) and the appendix proof (A_time_reversal.tex) collapse the posterior only when y_{n+1} is IN the conditioning set; the predictive p(X_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n}) is the y_{n+1}-average of p(x|k,y_{n+1}) and remains a genuine j-mixture. Decisive internal contradiction: the paper's own Step (II) (eqs (20)-(23)) uses pair-(j,k)-indexed innovation statistics, and Step (I) builds P_{n+1}(k) by summing over j (eq (18)) — impossible if p(Z_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n}) were a single Gaussian, so the l.226-228 claim ("Gaussian conditioning of the regime-conditional joint") is also wrong. (3) I independently re-derived the finding's repair: marginalizing the appendix collapse over r_{1:n-1} gives p(x_{n+1}|r_n=j, r_{n+1}=k, y_{1:n+1}) = p(x_{n+1}|r_{n+1}=k, y_{n+1}) for all j, so every mixture component shares the common linear-Gaussian conditional N(M_k y + c_k, G_k); then Σ_XY = M_k Σ_YY (so K = M_k), the mean update returns M_k y + c_k, and Σ_XX − K S Kᵀ = G_k by total variance — i.e., (27)-(31) applied to mixture moments are exact. This argument appears nowhere in the paper; the printed justification is the false statement. Conclusion of the paper stands (consistent with prior numerical validation of h5_exact), but the stated proof of the central exactness step is erroneous. Minor nit: the finding's sub-claim (i) is overstated as an absolute (densities with different conditioning CAN coincide — H5 itself is such an identity); the substantive point (generic failure, y_{n+1}-dependence mismatch) is correct.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep high for a paper-correctness audit: the only printed justification of the paper's headline exactness claim is a false mathematical statement (predictive equated to a posterior; non-Gaussian joint called Gaussian), in the section explicitly titled around exactness. However, note the formulas (27)-(31) and the algorithm are correct and the final result is unaffected — if the severity scale reserves "high" for incorrect results/formulas rather than incorrect proofs, downgrade to medium. The proposed fix (two-line argument: common component-wise conditional via (25)-(26) + total-variance/Σ_XY=KS identity) is the right repair.

verdicts.title: Eqs (21)-(23) : conditionnement \yn = y_{1:n} incohérent — Cov(Y_{n+1},Y_n|·,y_{1:n}) ≡ 0 et Σ_{YY,n}(j)^{-1} = 0^{-1} sous la boucle de la Remarque 4

verdict.reasoning: Every factual element verified against the sources. (1) paper/macros.tex l.64 defines \yn = y_{1:n}; paper/sections/03_filtering.tex ll.172-173 and l.181 condition the cross-covariances of eqs (22)-(23) on (j,k,\yn). (2) The paper's own definitions make the formulas degenerate: eq (11) (ll.35-37) defines (mu_n, P_n) as posterior moments given y_{1:n}; ll.184-187 explicitly state that all quantities in (21)-(23) come from these regime moments and their propagation; Remark 4 (ll.285-302) re-injects posterior moments whose centred YY block is exactly zero (Y_{n+1}=y_{n+1} fully observed, l.294). Hence centred Cov(Y_{n+1},Y_n|j,k,y_{1:n}) = 0 and Sigma_{YY,n}(j) = 0, so eq (23) is 0 * 0^{-1} — indefinite within the paper's own loop, and the regression correction in (21) vanishes identically. I tried two defenses and both fail: (a) reading \Covk literally as the raw moment E[Y_{n+1}Y_n^T|.] (macro definition) makes it non-zero but renders the regression formula mathematically wrong, and the paper itself uses the same macro as a CENTRED covariance at eq (13) (l.103-104: = F_k(P_n(j) - mu mu^T), no b_k mu^T term); (b) no announced convention exists — the only 'abuse of notation' disclaimer (l.137) concerns Sigma-blocks, and y_{1:n-1} appears nowhere in the paper. (3) The code reading is confirmed: prg/filter/gss_filter.py _update_step_general stores propagated (predicted) moments (self._mu = mu_np1, l.874), never applies Remark 4's posterior collapse, and computes the regression at ll.803-812 with non-degenerate moments; the .tex init (ll.361-366) feeds yet a third meaning (unconditional stationary moments), confirming the symbol-overload defect. (4) Re-derived by hand: under the predicted reading, M_tilde = C_k M_j + D_k and eq (21) = C_k xhat^{(j)} + D_k y_n + b_{Y,k}, matching mu_jk(y_n) of Proposition prop:markov (02_model_h5.tex ll.104-117) — mean exact under (H5), while the variance defect of (22) is the separately confirmed S_jk bug. The finding is real, precisely cited, and not an exaggeration: the central recursion of the paper is ill-defined as literally written from the second time step onward.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep high. The defect makes Step (II) of the paper's central recursion indefinite (inverse of a zero matrix) under the paper's own definitions and Remark 4, so a reader cannot execute the published algorithm as written. One calibration note: it shares eqs (21)-(23) with the already-confirmed S_jk variance bug (wave 1-2) — report it as a distinct notational/conditioning defect (wrong conditioning set y_{1:n} vs y_{1:n-1}, plus predicted/posterior symbol overload), not as a second independent numerical error, to avoid double-counting. The proposed fix (explicit predicted/posterior symbols, or posterior form yhat = C_k xhat + D_k y_n + b_{Y,k} with S = C_k P C_k^T + Sigma_V(k)) is correct and matches both prop:markov and the code's h5_exact Gamma (gss_filter.py ll.431-461).

verdicts.title: Eq. (22) : variance d'innovation de paire S^{(j,k)} fausse — mélange de conditionnements, contredit la Proposition 1

verdict.reasoning: Refutation attempted and failed on all axes. (1) Numbering verified: no numbered equations before 02_model_h5.tex, so eq (22)=eq:S_jk (03_filtering.tex:170-175), eq (11)=Gamma_jk, eqs (15)-(18)=step (I), (20)=y_transition, (24)=regime_post — all citations in the finding are exact. (2) The conditioning mismatch is real: eq:var_Z (lines 107-117) constructs P_{n+1}(k) by mixing over r_n=j with the reversed kernel (15), so Sigma_{YY,n+1}(k) — indexed by k alone, defined lines 126-140 — is the r_n-MARGINAL predictive variance, while the subtracted term in (22) is pair-(j,k)-conditional. Not a Schur complement of any single joint law; no PSD guarantee (code guards with _psd_floor at gss_filter.py:809). (3) Re-derivation confirms the exact variance: Var(Y_{n+1}|r_n=j,r_{n+1}=k,y_{1:n}) = C_k P_{n|n}^(j) C_k^T + Sigma_{V,k}, and under the AB constraint (A=Delta Sigma_V^-1 C, B=Delta Sigma_V^-1 D, prg/utils/h5_constraint.py) P_{n|n}^(j) = Sigma_{U,j} - Delta_j Sigma_{V,j}^-1 Delta_j^T, giving exactly Gamma_jk of eq (11) — n-invariant. (22) differs from Gamma_jk whenever regime moments differ across j, contradicting Prop. 1 and even Remark 1 (lines 56-59) of the same section, which claims S matches Gamma_jk in the stationary limit. (4) Additional incoherence: under the paper's own definition (14) (moments conditioned on y_{1:n}) plus Remark 4, Sigma_{YY,n}(j)=0 and (21)-(23) are 0/0-degenerate; only the predicted-moment reading (what the code does, storing mu_np1/P_np1 at lines 873-874) is well-defined, and then (22) is still j-mixed. Under no reading is (22) exact. (5) The paper presents the recursion as exact with no caveat (lines 14-15, 43-46, 217; Remark 1 lines 62-63 calls the general mode 'more reliable') and gives no derivation of (20)-(23). (6) Code confirmation: _update_step_general lines 793-809 implements the (22) structure; tellingly, the module docstring (lines 44-63) contains an 'IMM approximation note' admitting imm_general is approximate — an admission absent from the paper, strengthening the finding. Minor over-claims in the finding (the _psd_floor argument is weak alone since _psd_floor is generic hygiene elsewhere; the code's imm_general also deviates via filtered-pi mixing weights, so the numerical Delta-pi is not solely attributable to (22)) do not affect the verdict.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep critical. Eq (22) sits in the mode-probability update at the heart of the paper's central claim of exactness; it contaminates pi_{n+1} via eqs (20)/(24) and all downstream steps, contradicts Prop. 1 internally, and the numerically confirmed divergence under (H5) is large (max|Delta pi|~0.25-0.46). The fix is local (invoke Prop. 1: Lambda = N(mu_jk(y_n), Gamma_jk), or use the pair-conditional first term [F_k Sigma_n(j) F_k^T + Sigma_W(k)]_YY), but as written the main recursion of Section 3 is false while being advertised as exact.

verdicts.title: Éléments revérifiés et corrects : Γ_jk, pente de μ_jk, Remarque 3, caractérisation de (H4) ⇒ C = 0, dimensions/transposées

verdict.reasoning: This is an [info] traceability finding asserting that several checks PASS, and every assertion survives adversarial re-verification. (i) eq:Gamma_jk (02_model_h5.tex:113-116) re-derived by hand: under (H3) and the AB form of (H5) (matching prg/utils/h5_constraint.py, A=ΔΣ_V⁻¹C, B=ΔΣ_V⁻¹D), Var(X_n|r_n=j,y_n) equals the noise Schur complement Σ_{U,j}−Δ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹Δ_jᵀ, giving Γ_jk = Σ_{V,k}+C_k(Σ_{U,j}−Δ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹Δ_jᵀ)C_kᵀ exactly as printed, PSD by construction; confirmed by my own independent 4M-step MC (different model, seed, estimator) to 3-4 decimals on all (j,k) pairs. (ii) Slope of eq:mu_jk = D_k+C_kΔ_jΣ_{V,j}⁻¹ confirmed analytically and by MC to 4 decimals; the finding correctly endorses only the linear part (my MC confirms the intercept as printed is incomplete when C≠0, consistent with a separate finding). (iii) Remark 3 (lines 132-138) true: rerunning MC with different biases leaves Γ unchanged. (iv) (H4)⇒C=0 (lines 32-39) correct: mean C_{r_{n+1}}x_n+Dy_n+b_Y independent of x_n over full Gaussian support iff C=0; bib entries derrode_cgomsm_2013 (Derrode-Pieczynski SPL 2013) and abbassi_cgpmsm_2015 (Abbassi-Benmiloud-Pieczynski TAC 2015) verified in paper.bib. (v) All dimensions/transposes consistent (Δ q×s, C s×q, b_r∈R^{q+s} line 67) with macros.tex and code shapes. (vi) eq:mix_mean/eq:mix_var (lines 148-158) are the laws of total expectation/variance, structurally correct; the \Vark macro defect (macros.tex:26 defines it as uncentered E[XXᵀ|·]) is real but explicitly carved out by the finding. Line references in the finding are accurate. The finding's own MC numbers (0.5894 vs 0.5960, ~1% off-diagonal deviations) are plausible MC noise for rarer regime-pair transitions and are superseded by my tighter independent MC, which agrees with theory. Minor nuance: the formulas are exact under the AB parametrisation; in the sub-determined regime Ks<q+s non-AB (H5) models could differ, but the proposition is delegated to the companion paper, so this does not invalidate the finding.
verdict.severityAdjustment: None — [info] is the right level for a positive traceability/verification record; keep as is.

verdicts.title: Macro \Vark = E[AAᵀ|·] (moment d'ordre 2) utilisée comme variance centrée : eq:mix_var est fausse telle qu'affichée

verdict.reasoning:
CORE CLAIM CONFIRMED. macros.tex:26 defines \Vark{A}{c} -> E[A Aᵀ | c], a raw (uncentred) second moment, under the contradictory comment "Auto-covariance" (l. 25). eq:mix_var (sections/02_model_h5.tex:152-157) has the law-of-total-variance structure: \Vark{X_{n+1}}{\ynp} = Σ_k π_k [\Vark{X_{n+1}}{k,\ynp} + spread·spreadᵀ]. After macro expansion the PDF literally displays E[XXᵀ|y_{1:n+1}] = Σ_k π_k E[XXᵀ|k,y] + Σ_k π_k (μ_k−μ)(μ_k−μ)ᵀ, which contradicts the tower property (E[XXᵀ|y] = Σ_k π_k E[XXᵀ|k,y] exactly; the spread term is double-counted). The rendered equation is mathematically false; only the intended centred-variance version is correct.

ADVERSARIAL CHECKS ALL FAIL TO REFUTE: (1) No announced convention rescues it — the Notation paragraph (01_introduction.tex:120-131) defines \Ek and \pk but never declares E[AAᵀ|·] to denote a centred covariance; the only "abuse of notation" remark (03_filtering.tex:137-139) covers the Σ-blocks of P_{n+1}(k), not \Vark. (2) It is not a consistent editorial shorthand either — the paper provably honors the raw-moment semantics elsewhere: 03_filtering.tex:37 deliberately uses \Ek{Z_n Z_nᵀ}{·} for the raw moment P_n(k), and 03_filtering.tex:337 manually centres inside the macro (\Vark{Z_1 − μ_{z_0}(k)}{r_1=k}) when a true covariance is wanted. So \Vark genuinely means raw moment in some places and is misused as centred variance in others. (3) The mixed usage is more pervasive than the finding states: 03_filtering.tex:32 defines P_{n|n}^{(k)} := \Vark{X_n}{r_n=k,\yn} and calls it a "covariance" (it is then used as centred covariance in eq:kalman_var/eq:combine_var); appendix/E_joseph.tex:57 writes the Joseph identity with \Vark on the LHS (false as rendered, since E[XXᵀ|k,y] ≠ (I−KH)Σ(I−KH)ᵀ+KRKᵀ unless the posterior mean is zero); appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:70 uses \Vark{Y_{n+1}}{j,k} as a covariance block of the Gaussian-conditioning matrix Ω_jk while E[Y_{n+1}|j,k] = b_{Y,k} ≠ 0 in general.

ONE SUB-CLAIM OVERSTATED: "E[W_{n+1}|r_{n+1}]=0 jamais énoncé" is not quite right — sections/05_estimation.tex:20 states w_{n+1} ~ N(0, Σ_{W,k}). It is stated late (Sec. 5, in the supervised-OLS context) rather than at the model definition in Sec. 2 where eq:noise_cov appears, and the bias b_r being split out makes zero-mean W the standard reading anyway; eq:noise_cov is effectively fine. This sub-claim should be softened to "stated only in Sec. 5, not where the model is defined".

IMPACT: purely notational/typesetting — the algorithm, the centred-Σ filter equations, and the code (prg/filter/gss_filter.py uses proper centred covariances) are unaffected. But a methodology paper displaying a literally false foundational decomposition (eq:mix_var), plus a false-as-rendered Joseph identity in Appendix E, is exactly what a referee flags. The proposed fix (a \Varc centred-variance macro) is correct and cheap.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep medium. The error is notational with zero algorithmic impact, but it is pervasive (eq:mix_var, 03_filtering.tex:32, appendix E eq:joseph_proof l. 57, appendix B l. 70) and yields several displayed equations that are literally false under the paper's own demonstrated macro semantics. Drop or downgrade the eq:noise_cov/W-zero-mean sub-claim: w_{n+1} ~ N(0, Σ_{W,k}) is stated at 05_estimation.tex:20, just not in Sec. 2.

verdicts.title: Macro \Vark = E[AAᵀ|·] (moment d'ordre 2) utilisée comme variance centrée : eq:mix_var est fausse telle qu'affichée

verdict.reasoning: Verified by independent rederivation. macros.tex l. 26 defines \Vark{A}{c} as E[A Aᵀ|c] (raw second moment; no redefinition anywhere in paper/). eq:mix_var (02_model_h5.tex l. 152-157) therefore typesets as E[XXᵀ|y] = Σₖ πₖ[E[XXᵀ|k,y] + (μₖ−μ)(μₖ−μ)ᵀ], which is false: the tower property gives E[XXᵀ|y] = Σₖ πₖ E[XXᵀ|k,y] with no spread term, so the displayed RHS overcounts by Σₖ πₖ(μₖ−μ)(μₖ−μ)ᵀ ⪰ 0 (nonzero whenever regime-conditional means differ). The equation is the law of total variance, valid only for centered variances. The mixed-semantics claim is confirmed and even reinforced: 03_filtering.tex l. 32 uses \Vark as a centered 'covariance' (consistent with eq:kalman_var), l. 37 deliberately uses the raw moment E[ZZᵀ|·] (cf. l. 363, P_1(k)=Σ+μμᵀ), and l. 337 manually centers inside \Vark (\Vark{Z_1−μ_{z0}(k)}{r_1=k}), proving the authors know the macro is raw yet did not center in eq:mix_var. One sub-claim is overstated: E[W|r]=0 is not 'never stated' — 05_estimation.tex l. 20 states w_{n+1} ~ N(0, Σ_{W,k}) — but it is indeed absent from Sec. 2 (model definition, eq:noise_cov) and Sec. 3 (eq:var_Z relies on it implicitly), so the substance stands in weakened form.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep medium. It is a genuinely false displayed equation plus inconsistent notation visible to any careful reviewer, but purely presentational: the actual recursion (eq:combine_var uses centered Kalman covariances) and the code are correct, so there is no algorithmic consequence. Soften the secondary eq:noise_cov claim from 'never stated' to 'not stated in Secs. 2-3 where it is used (only in Sec. 5, l. 20, in the estimation context)'.

verdicts.title: Sélection multi-restart sur la vraisemblance PRÉ-projection : le papier décrit fidèlement le code mais n'avertit jamais que l_hat ne correspond pas au modèle retourné

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line checks out. Paper: 05_estimation.tex l.129 ("restart with the highest converged log-likelihood is retained"), Alg. 2 l.183 (argmax over ℓ) before l.184-186 (AB projection on θ̂), and neither Sec. 5.2.4 (l.131-140, discusses only monotonicity vs GEM) nor Appendix D warns that the retained ℓ is not the likelihood of the returned projected model, or that restart ranking may differ under the constrained family. Code: semi_supervised.py records log_lik during EM (l.528, l.643) before the post-hoc projection (l.589-616, inside _em_run), selects restarts on this pre-projection value (l.779), and embeds it in the generated model docstring (l.952-960) for a saved post-projection model. The finding's parenthetical correction about projection happening inside _em_run before selection is accurate, and the final returned model matches Alg. 2 since projection is deterministic. Additional corroboration the finding missed: run_real_data.py l.286 stores train_LL = info["best_log_lik"] (pre-projection), and tab_enso_em.tex reports identical "train log L̂ = −437.4" for V0 and V1; 07_real_data.tex l.153-157 even highlights this equality — an artifact of labeling the pre-projection likelihood as the constrained variant's training likelihood, while the projected model's actual likelihood is never computed anywhere. The mathematical claims (projection generically changes likelihood, typically lowering it from a local EM optimum; unconstrained ranking need not match post-projection ranking) are sound. Counterpoint considered: Alg. 2 transparently shows the mechanism, but the finding correctly frames the issue as a missing caveat plus a misleading reported quantity, not a misdescription of the algorithm.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep medium. The core theory (exact H5 filtering) is unaffected, but the issue is stronger than a pure documentation nit: the paper's Table tab_enso_em and the text at 07_real_data.tex l.153-157 present the pre-projection likelihood as the V1 (post-hoc AB) variant's training log-likelihood and draw a conclusion from its equality with V0. Medium, on the firm side.

verdicts.title: « Under (H1)–(H3) and (H5), the joint dynamics admit a fully coupled state-space representation » : (H5) n'y joue aucun rôle

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the real .tex. (1) The quoted sentence is exactly at paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex:56-58, introducing eq:dynamics (l.61) and eq:noise_cov (l.76). (2) Re-derivation: (H1)+(H2) give a conditionally Gaussian kernel with affine mean, and the kernel form of (H3) collapses the (r_n,r_{n+1}) dependence to r_{n+1}, yielding eq:dynamics–eq:noise_cov; no step uses (H5), and (H5) (which only zeroes the partial regression of X_{n+1} on y_n given (r_{n+1},y_{n+1})) cannot supply existence, Gaussianity, or r_{n+1}-homogeneity of the representation. (3) The paper's own logic confirms this: 04_constraint.tex ll.7-10 says (H5) 'imposes an algebraic relation between the seven matrices that parameterise a single regime' (representation pre-exists); appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex Step 1 invokes only (H2)-(H3) and assumes 'the dynamics eq:dynamics–eq:noise_cov' before 'imposing (H5)' at Step 3 (l.87); and the 'No (H5)' row of the table in 04_constraint.tex (ll.111-114) describes the same seven-block representation with no constraint, proving the form exists without (H5). (4) Adversarial defenses fail: 'fully coupled' (C_r ≠ 0) comes from dropping (H4), not from adding (H5); and while the sentence is not strictly false (adding a hypothesis to a true implication keeps it true), it does misattribute (H5) a role in the existence of the state-space form, opposite to its actual role of constraining the blocks. The proposed fix is accurate.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep at low. It is a wording/attribution imprecision in exposition, not a mathematical error — a one-line fix with no impact on any derivation or result. Low is the correct floor; do not raise.

verdicts.title: Régimes dégénérés : pour (mu_z0, Sigma_z0) le papier annonce un reset, le code conserve les valeurs précédentes

verdict.reasoning: All citations verified against the real sources. (1) paper/appendix/D_baum_welch.tex:105-112 states verbatim that for empty regimes the M-step is skipped and (F_k, b_k, Sigma_W,k) are "reset to identity dynamics with unit noise covariance", and that "The same convention applies to (mu_z0,k, Sigma_z0,k)". Appendix D (lines 8-9) explicitly documents Algorithm alg:semi_supervised, so prg/learning/semi_supervised.py is the right comparison. (2) semi_supervised.py:297-302 confirms the reset for F=I, b=0, SigW=I (unit covariance; the "large noise" comment is indeed inaccurate but harmless). (3) semi_supervised.py:576-584 confirms the mu_z0/Sigma_z0 update is guarded by 'if denom_k > _LOG_FLOOR:' with NO else branch — previous-iteration (or initialization) values are retained, never reset; they flow unchanged into the returned params (lines 638-639) with no downstream reset. The paper's "same convention" claim is therefore inaccurate: dynamics get skip-and-reset, initial moments get skip-and-retain. Additional unmentioned support: the floors differ too (floor_w=1e-12 at line 276 vs _LOG_FLOOR=1e-300 at line 89). The only possible refutation — reading "same convention" as referring only to the skip — fails because the sentence defines the convention as skip AND reset. The proposed fix correctly describes the code behavior.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Keep [low]. It is a paper/code consistency error in an appendix describing degenerate edge-case handling: no mathematical error, observable behavioral difference only when a regime collapses to zero weight during EM. The fix is a one-sentence wording change in App D (or a two-line else-branch in the code if reset semantics are preferred).

verdicts.title: Remarque 1 : la factorisation est triviale et la gaussianité de p(x_n, y_n | r_n) est fausse en général

verdict.reasoning:
La trouvaille repose sur une mauvaise lecture de (H3). Son argument central — « p(x_n, y_n | r_n) est un mélange de gaussiennes à K^{n−1} composantes ; aucune des hypothèses (H1)–(H3), (H5) ne le réduit à une gaussienne » — ignore la SECONDE équation de (H3) (paper/sections/02_model_h5.tex, lignes 21–26) : p(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1} | r_n, r_{n+1}) = p(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1} | r_{n+1}). C'est précisément l'hypothèse qui rend toutes les composantes du mélange identiques.

Dérivation refaite à la main : (i) Base — par H1 (triplet Markov) + H3(première équation), p(r_{m+1} | z_m, r_m) = p(r_{m+1} | r_m), et en marginalisant z_m on obtient p(r_{m+1} | z_1, r_{1:m}) = p(r_{m+1} | r_m), donc z_1 ⊥ r_{2:N} | r_1 et p(z_1 | r_1) = p(z_1 | r_{1:N}), gaussienne par H2. (ii) Récurrence — p(z_n | r_{1:n}) = ∫ p(z_n | z_{n−1}, r_{n−1}, r_n) p(z_{n−1} | r_{n−1}) dz_{n−1} = p(z_n | r_{n−1}, r_n) : noyau affine-gaussien (issu de H2) appliqué à une gaussienne, donc gaussienne ; puis = p(z_n | r_n) par H3(seconde équation). Conclusion : sous (H1)–(H3) (H5 même pas nécessaire), p(x_n, y_n | r_n) EST une gaussienne unique, exactement ce qu'affirme la Remarque 1.
